sections.heading: Overview of the Publishing Process
sections.content: Publishing an Android app to Google Play Store is a multi-step process that involves creating a developer account, preparing your app for release, building and signing a release artifact, creating a store listing, and rolling out to users through a structured release track system. Google Play uses Android App Bundles (AAB) as the required upload format for new apps — Google's servers then generate optimized APKs tailored to each device's screen density, CPU architecture, and language. The Play Console is Google's web-based dashboard for managing every aspect of your app's presence on Google Play — from release management and store listings to crash reports, ratings, revenue, and policy compliance. A well-prepared release takes time but ensures a smooth review process and a positive first impression for users.

rows.tasks: Create Google Play Developer account; pay one-time $25 registration fee; complete identity verification; accept Developer Distribution Agreement
Create new app in Play Console; set app name, default language, app or game type, free or paid status
rows.where: Play Console > All apps > Create app

rows.tasks: Write title, short description, full description; upload screenshots, feature graphic, app icon; add categorization and contact details
rows.where: Play Console > Store presence > Main store listing

rows.tasks: Complete content rating questionnaire; set privacy policy URL; declare app content, target audience, data safety
Build release AAB with ./gradlew bundleRelease; sign with upload key; enable Google Play App Signing
Upload AAB to Internal Testing track; promote through Closed Testing > Open Testing > Production; set rollout percentage
Submit for review; wait for Google review (hours to days); address policy violations if any; app goes live

sections.heading: Setting Up a Google Play Developer Account
items: Go to play.google.com/console and sign in with a Google account dedicated to your developer or company identity
Pay the one-time $25 USD registration fee — this is a lifetime fee, not annual
Complete identity verification — Google requires phone verification and may request additional identity documents for new accounts
For organizations, use a Google account associated with your business rather than a personal account
Accept the Google Play Developer Distribution Agreement before you can publish any apps
Set up your developer profile: public developer name (shown on Play Store), email address, website, and phone number
Enable two-factor authentication on your Google account — your Play Console account controls app publishing and payments
Add team members with specific permissions: Play Console > Users and permissions — assign roles like Release Manager, Financial Reports, Developer

rows.requirement: Up to 30 characters — shown as the app name on the Play Store listing page
rows.tips: Include your brand name; keep it clear and recognizable; avoid keyword stuffing

rows.requirement: Up to 80 characters — shown in search results and app listing preview
rows.tips: Summarize the core value proposition in one sentence; most important real estate after the title

rows.requirement: Up to 4000 characters — shown on the full app listing page below the screenshots
rows.tips: First 167 characters are shown without expanding; put the most important information first; use formatting with line breaks

rows.requirement: 512x512 PNG with no alpha transparency — shown on the Play Store listing
rows.tips: Must match the launcher icon in the APK; use a clean design that looks good at small sizes

rows.requirement: 1024x500 PNG or JPEG — shown at the top of the listing when the app is featured
rows.tips: Required for featuring opportunities; use simple, bold visuals without small text

rows.requirement: Minimum 2, maximum 8 per device type — phone, 7-inch tablet, 10-inch tablet, Chromebook, TV, Wear OS
rows.tips: Show core features; use device frames or lifestyle images; phone screenshots are required; others are optional but recommended

rows.requirement: Optional — YouTube video URL; shown at the top of the listing alongside the feature graphic
rows.tips: 30-120 seconds recommended; show the app in action; must be publicly available on YouTube

sections.heading: Preparing the Release Build
items: Ensure versionCode is incremented from the previous release — Play Store rejects uploads with duplicate or lower versionCode
Update versionName to a human-readable string for the new release: versionName = "2.1.0"
Enable R8 minification and resource shrinking in the release build type: isMinifyEnabled = true; isShrinkResources = true
Configure signing with your release keystore or upload key for Google Play App Signing
Build the release AAB: ./gradlew bundleRelease — output at build/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab
Test the release AAB locally before uploading: use bundletool to extract APKs and install on a device
Install bundletool: download from github.com/google/bundletool/releases
Build APK set from AAB: java -jar bundletool.jar build-apks --bundle=app-release.aab --output=app.apks --connected-device
Install on connected device: java -jar bundletool.jar install-apks --apks=app.apks
Verify the release build works end-to-end: test all critical user flows in the release build before uploading

rows.audience: Up to 100 testers added by email — must be Google accounts
rows.review: No Google review — available to testers within minutes of upload
rows.use_case: Quick smoke testing by your development team immediately after upload
rows.promotion: Can be promoted to Closed Testing, Open Testing, or Production

rows.audience: Specific testers via Google Groups or Google Play Groups — unlimited testers
rows.review: Brief Google review required before testers can access
rows.use_case: Controlled beta testing with a specific group of external testers
rows.promotion: Can be promoted to Open Testing or Production

rows.audience: Any Google Play user who joins the beta program — publicly visible opt-in
rows.use_case: Public beta testing — users self-select to test pre-release builds; reviews appear separately from production

rows.audience: All Google Play users in the selected countries
rows.review: Full Google review required — typically hours to a few days for new apps
rows.use_case: Live release to all users — supports percentage-based staged rollouts
rows.promotion: Final destination — halt or rollback available if issues are found

sections.heading: Staged Rollouts
items: Staged rollout releases the new version to a percentage of production users — the rest continue receiving the previous version
Start with a small percentage: 1% or 5% — monitor crash rates and ANR rates in Android Vitals before expanding
Expand the rollout gradually: 5% > 20% > 50% > 100% — wait at least 24-48 hours between expansions to observe metrics
Halt a rollout: if crash rates spike, halt the rollout immediately — users on the new version are unaffected but no more users receive it
Resume a halted rollout: fix the issue, upload a new AAB with an incremented versionCode, then resume or restart the rollout
Full rollout: set percentage to 100% when you are confident in the release quality
Users not yet on the new version will be updated when they open the Play Store or when the device checks for updates
Staged rollouts are not available for apps with fewer than a minimum number of active users — Play Console will indicate if staged rollout is available

rows.description: Complete the IARC questionnaire to receive age ratings for all countries — required before publishing
rows.location: Play Console > Policy > App content > Content rating

rows.description: URL to your app's privacy policy — required for all apps; mandatory if your app collects any user data
rows.location: Play Console > Policy > App content > Privacy policy

rows.description: Declare what data your app collects, why it is collected, and whether it is shared with third parties — shown to users on the store listing
rows.location: Play Console > Policy > App content > Data safety

rows.description: Specify the age group your app targets — apps targeting children under 13 must comply with Families Policy and additional restrictions
rows.location: Play Console > Policy > App content > Target audience and content

rows.description: Declare whether your app contains ads — must match the actual app content; misleading declarations violate Play policy
rows.location: Play Console > Policy > App content > Ads

rows.description: Provide test credentials or instructions if your app requires login — helps Google reviewers access all app content
rows.location: Play Console > Policy > App content > App access

sections.heading: Data Safety Section
items: The Data Safety section is shown prominently on your app's Play Store listing — users see it before downloading
Declare all data types your app collects: location, personal info, financial info, health data, messages, files, contacts, app activity, device identifiers
For each data type declare: whether it is collected, whether it is shared with third parties, whether collection is required or optional, and the purpose
Third-party SDKs collect data too — audit all SDKs in your app (Firebase, Analytics, Ads) and include their data collection in your declaration
Data safety must be accurate — inaccurate declarations violate Google Play policy and can result in app removal
Google provides a Data Safety Help Center with guidance on how to categorize each type of data
Update the Data Safety section whenever you add new SDKs or change what data your app collects
Inconsistency between your privacy policy and Data Safety section is a common policy violation — keep them in sync

rows.description: Google securely stores the app signing key and re-signs your AAB after upload — you sign with the upload key only
rows.action: Enroll during first AAB upload — mandatory for new apps; Play Console guides you through the process

rows.topic: Upload key vs App signing key
rows.description: Upload key: you hold this; used to sign AABs before upload. App signing key: Google holds this; used to sign APKs delivered to users
rows.action: Sign your release AAB with your upload keystore before uploading to Play Console

rows.description: Google provides SHA-1 and SHA-256 fingerprints of the app signing key — needed for Firebase, Google Maps API, and App Links
rows.action: Play Console > Setup > App integrity > App signing tab — copy the fingerprints for use in Firebase and API configurations

rows.description: If your upload key is lost or compromised, contact Google Play support to reset it — the app signing key is unaffected
rows.action: Generate a new upload key and submit a key reset request through Play Console with identity verification

rows.description: The app signing key SHA-256 fingerprint must be in your assetlinks.json file for verified App Links to work in production
rows.action: After enrolling in Play App Signing, update assetlinks.json with the SHA-256 from the App signing tab — not from your local keystore

sections.heading: Versioning Strategy
items: versionCode — integer used by the Play Store to identify build order; must be incremented with every upload; users never see this value
versionName — human-readable string shown to users: '2.1.0', '2.1.0-beta1'; can be any string
Use semantic versioning for versionName: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH — e.g. 2.1.0 where major = breaking changes, minor = new features, patch = bug fixes
Automate versionCode to avoid forgetting to increment: set it from the CI build number or use a Gradle task to read it from a file
versionCode automation example: versionCode = System.getenv("BUILD_NUMBER")?.toInt() ?: 1
For multi-flavor apps, each flavor can have its own versionCode: productFlavors { free { versionCode = 1000 + rootVersionCode }; paid { versionCode = 2000 + rootVersionCode } }
Play Store allows up to 2,100,000,000 for versionCode — start at 1 and increment by 1 for each release
Never reuse or lower a versionCode — Play Store will reject the upload with 'Version code already used'

rows.purpose: Monitors app stability — crash rate, ANR rate, excessive wakeups, stuck partial wake locks
rows.why_important: Poor vitals can result in Play Store warnings, reduced visibility, and a 'contains bugs' badge on your listing

rows.purpose: Dashboard showing the current state of all release tracks and any pending reviews
rows.why_important: Central view for tracking what version is live on each track and the status of pending releases

rows.purpose: App metrics — installs, uninstalls, ratings, crashes, active devices, retention over time
rows.why_important: Understanding growth trends, uninstall causes, and which devices your app performs worst on

rows.purpose: User ratings and reviews — reply to reviews directly from Play Console
rows.why_important: Responding to reviews improves user trust and can prevent negative word-of-mouth; monitor for usability issues

rows.purpose: Inspect generated APK splits from an uploaded AAB — see exactly what APK each device configuration receives
rows.why_important: Verify APK size for specific device configurations; download mapping.txt and native debug symbols

rows.purpose: Shows any policy violations or warnings on your app — enforcement actions and required fixes
rows.why_important: Policy violations can suspend the app or your entire developer account — resolve immediately

sections.heading: Pre-Launch Report
items: Google automatically runs your app on real devices in a Firebase Test Lab environment after each AAB upload to any track
The pre-launch report checks for: crashes, ANRs, broken permissions, display issues across screen sizes, and accessibility problems
Access the report: Play Console > Android Vitals > Pre-launch report — available within a few hours of upload
The report includes screenshots of your app running on multiple device configurations — useful for catching layout issues
Crashes found in the pre-launch report are shown with stack traces — fix them before promoting to wider tracks
Accessibility checks identify missing content descriptions, small touch targets, and low contrast — address these to improve your app's accessibility score
Security checks flag common vulnerabilities — plain HTTP usage, exported components without permissions
The pre-launch report does not block publishing — it is informational; you can promote to Production even with warnings

rows.description: App title, description, screenshots, or icon do not accurately represent the app's functionality
rows.fix: Ensure all store listing assets accurately represent what the app does — no false claims or misleading imagery

rows.description: App requests sensitive permissions or collects user data but has no privacy policy URL declared
rows.fix: Add a privacy policy URL in Play Console under App content > Privacy policy — the policy must be accessible and cover the app's data practices

rows.description: The Data Safety declaration does not match the app's actual data collection and sharing practices
rows.fix: Audit all SDKs and APIs; update the Data Safety section to accurately reflect all data collected and shared

rows.description: App requests permissions (CAMERA, RECORD_AUDIO, READ_CONTACTS) that are not necessary for its core functionality
rows.fix: Remove unused permissions; if permissions are needed, ensure the app's purpose clearly justifies them in the listing

rows.description: The app crashes during Google's review process — reviewers cannot complete the review flow
rows.fix: Test the release build thoroughly; check the pre-launch report; fix all crashes before resubmitting

rows.description: App uses trademarks, copyrighted assets, or impersonates another app or brand
rows.fix: Use only original or licensed assets; do not use another app's name, icon, or branding

rows.description: App content violates Google Play policies — hate speech, adult content in a non-adult app, gambling without authorization
rows.fix: Review the Google Play Developer Policy Center; remove or restrict violating content; use content ratings appropriately

sections.heading: Localized Store Listings
items: Provide translated store listings for each language your app supports — increases discoverability in non-English markets
Play Console > Store presence > Store listing > Manage translations — add translations for title, short description, and full description
Use Google's managed translations service in Play Console for professional translations — paid service integrated directly
Or manage translations manually: export strings, send to translators, import translated files
Provide localized screenshots for major markets — screenshots in the local language perform significantly better
The default language listing is shown to users whose language has no specific translation — make the default listing high quality
Custom store listings allow different listings for different countries — useful for region-specific branding or features
Play Console > Store presence > Custom store listings — override the default listing for specific countries

rows.target: Below 1% crash-free sessions — Play Store may add a warning badge above this threshold
rows.action: Set up Crashlytics for detailed crash reports with stack traces; fix high-impact crashes in a hotfix release

rows.target: Below 0.47% ANR rate — Play Store threshold for poor behavior warning
rows.action: Profile main thread work with CPU Profiler; move blocking operations off the main thread

rows.target: 4.0+ average rating — below 3.0 significantly reduces install conversion
rows.action: Respond to negative reviews; use in-app review API to prompt satisfied users to rate

rows.target: Varies by category — benchmark against similar apps in your category
rows.action: A/B test store listing assets using Play Console's Store listing experiments feature

rows.target: Low and stable — spikes after an update indicate a regression
rows.action: Correlate uninstall spikes with specific releases; investigate crash reports and reviews from that period

sections.heading: In-App Review API
items: The In-App Review API lets you prompt users to rate and review your app without leaving it — shown as a system overlay
Add dependency: implementation("com.google.android.play:review:2.x.x"); implementation("com.google.android.play:review-ktx:2.x.x")
Create a ReviewManager: val reviewManager = ReviewManagerFactory.create(context)
Request review info: val request = reviewManager.requestReviewFlow(); request.addOnCompleteListener { task -> if (task.isSuccessful) launchReviewFlow(task.result) }
Launch the review flow: reviewManager.launchReviewFlow(activity, reviewInfo)
The system decides whether to show the dialog — it may not always be shown; Google rate-limits the prompt per user
Request the review at a meaningful moment: after the user completes a key task, not at app launch or during a critical flow
Never gate the prompt on a predicted positive rating — this violates Play policy; show the prompt to all users equally
Test using the FakeReviewManager in debug builds: val reviewManager = FakeReviewManager(context) — always shows the dialog

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Publishing to Google Play requires careful preparation across multiple dimensions: a complete and accurate store listing with high-quality screenshots and a compelling description, a correctly configured release build signed with your upload key, complete app content declarations including the Data Safety section and content rating, and a structured rollout strategy using release tracks. Start every release on the Internal Testing track, test thoroughly with bundletool, then promote through Closed and Open Testing before a staged Production rollout. Monitor Android Vitals for crash and ANR rates after each release, respond to user reviews promptly, and use the In-App Review API to collect ratings organically. Keep your Data Safety section, privacy policy, and store listing accurate and up to date — policy compliance is an ongoing responsibility, not a one-time checkbox.
